stories.headline: Denver mayor signs executive order restricting ICE activity, directing police to detain agents using excessive force
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston signed an executive order Thursday intended to protect protesters demonstrating against U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement operations in the event federal agents surge into the city. The order directs Denver Police Department officers to intervene and detain ICE agents observed using excessive force, and bans federal agents from using city-owned property — including overnight shelters, community centers, libraries and the Denver Coliseum — for their operations.

Johnston said his goal was not to provoke President Donald Trump, adding that he had no specific intelligence indicating an influx of ICE agents was imminent. He cited the situation in Minneapolis, where thousands of federal officers were deployed in recent months and two people were killed in separate incidents involving federal agents, as context for the order.

Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas and City Attorney Miko Brown stood alongside Johnston at the announcement. Brown described the order as constitutionally sound, saying that 'setting clear legal boundaries is not an act of defiance, it's responsible governance.' A spokesperson for the Department of Homeland Security did not immediately respond to a request for comment.

The order also states that DPD will take responsibility for conducting investigations of reported legal violations during protests. Johnston framed the measure as a necessary response to residents' questions about the city's posture should federal immigration enforcement operations expand to Denver.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis moves to permanently block ICE subpoena for immigrant sponsors' employment records
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis has filed a motion to permanently end his nine-month effort to comply with a federal immigration subpoena, arguing that ICE no longer appears to need employment records it sought roughly a year ago. Attorneys representing Polis informed a Denver judge Friday that the governor is seeking a permanent injunction that would indefinitely block the release of employment records for sponsors of unaccompanied immigrant children lacking proper legal status.

The move would effectively conclude a lawsuit filed by former state employee Scott Moss, who sued Polis last year to block compliance with the subpoena. Polis' legal team argued that ICE had not intervened in the suit nor taken any action to enforce the subpoena, suggesting the agency either no longer needs the information or considers it no longer urgent.

'We are asking the court to end this matter so we can move on,' said Polis spokesman Eric Maruyama. The state's Department of Labor and Employment has since shifted its policy to block similar subpoenas without a court order, and agency officials told lawmakers in January they had not received any ICE subpoenas since last summer.

Moss' attorney Laura Wolf said her team was 'in the midst of responding substantively' to the motion, noting they had insufficient time to consider the request before it was presented to the court. The state employees union Colorado WINS and the nonprofit law firm Towards Justice had also joined the litigation opposing the governor's attempted cooperation with ICE.

stories.headline: Tren de Aragua prosecutions in Colorado have yielded only plea deals despite aggressive rhetoric
stories.summary:
A series of sting operations launched in Colorado in late 2024 targeting alleged Tren de Aragua gang members has produced only plea deals rather than the sweeping prosecutions that officials publicly touted, according to CPR News. The operations began after the Arapahoe County Sheriff asked federal agents to help investigate an apartment complex in Arapahoe County where crime had spiked.

The indictments announced in the summer of 2025 drew significant attention in part because Tren de Aragua became a central focus of President Trump's 2024 campaign, including a high-profile rally at Aurora's Gaylord Rockies hotel. However, the legal proceedings that followed have not matched the scale of the enforcement rhetoric.

The outcomes have raised questions about the gap between political messaging around the Venezuelan gang and the evidentiary basis of the cases brought in Colorado courts. Plea deals, while resulting in convictions, typically involve reduced charges compared to the original indictments.

The cases remain significant to Aurora and Arapahoe County residents who saw crime rise in affected apartment complexes and who followed the national attention that the gang's alleged presence in Colorado attracted in 2024.

stories.headline: Missing Indigenous Person Alert issued for 13-year-old boy last seen in Denver's West Colfax neighborhood
stories.summary:
Colorado Bureau of Investigation issued a Missing Indigenous Person Alert Friday for Elias Olivas, a 13-year-old boy last seen at approximately 4:15 p.m. near West 13th Avenue and Lowell Boulevard in Denver's West Colfax neighborhood. Olivas is described as 5 feet 11 inches tall, 180 pounds, with brown hair and brown eyes, and is a member of the Oglala Sioux tribe.

He was last seen wearing a black hoodie with a large white cross on the back, blue jeans, black shoes and carrying a white Nike bag. Denver Police Department is leading the search.

Anyone with information about Olivas' whereabouts is asked to call 911 or the Denver Police Department at 720-913-2000. The Missing Indigenous Person Alert designation reflects the disproportionate rate at which Indigenous individuals go missing and the specialized response protocols tied to such cases in Colorado.

stories.headline: Denver cannabis lounge Tetra fined $10,000 in city's first marijuana hospitality licensing discipline case
stories.summary:
Denver's Department of Licensing and Consumer Protection has fined Tetra Lounge, a cannabis hospitality business at 3039 Walnut St., $10,000 following a November hearing over alleged violations during special events held on April 20 and July 10 of last year. The case marks the first licensing discipline action against a marijuana hospitality business in Denver, according to department spokesperson Eric Escudero.

City inspectors alleged they witnessed unlawful marijuana sales and sampling on the premises during the two events, consumption occurring inside areas not covered by the licensed premise, and vendors distributing marijuana products without authorization. Inspectors also reported the presence of booths selling psychedelic substances including psilocybin and DMT, which remain illegal. One city inspector lost their position after consuming a mushroom gummy while on the job during the April 20 inspection.

The business has been placed on a one-year probationary period. A subsequent violation would trigger an additional $10,000 fine and a 60-day closure. Tetra Lounge owner Dewayne Benjamin denied the allegations and said he plans to appeal the decision, adding that media coverage of the enforcement action has caused declines in event bookings, sponsorships and daily visitors.

Tetra Lounge, which opened in 2018, operates as a bring-your-own cannabis venue where patrons pay an entry fee to smoke on a licensed outdoor patio. Sales of cannabis products are not permitted under the terms of its license.

stories.headline: Colorado bill aims to clear legal path for affordable 'plug-in solar' panels for homes and apartments
stories.summary:
Colorado legislators are advancing House Bill 1007, which would remove regulatory barriers to portable 'plug-in solar' panels — small units roughly the size of a full-length mirror that connect directly to home electrical outlets or home batteries. The bill, co-sponsored by Reps. Lesley Smith of Boulder and Rebekah Stewart of Lakewood and Edgewater, would prohibit utilities from imposing unreasonable conditions on installation and would not require consumers to obtain prior approval from electric providers.

Proponents say the technology, already widespread in Germany where 1 million residents had installed similar devices by June of last year, offers a low-cost entry point for solar energy — particularly for renters and apartment dwellers who cannot install rooftop systems. A plug-in system with a storage battery is expected to retail for approximately $2,000 once the Colorado market opens following the bill's anticipated passage.

Namaste Solar co-owner Jason Sharpe, who demonstrated a beta plug-in system for legislators at the Capitol rotunda Wednesday, said plug-in solar and home battery storage are 'the most exciting areas in clean energy,' particularly as federal tax credits for larger installations face elimination. Approximately 30 states are currently advancing similar legislation.

A second provision of the bill would facilitate so-called 'meter collar' installations, allowing a plug-in connection to a home's existing electric meter for a two-way grid connection. Supporters cited growing consumer demand and Utah's recent passage of comparable legislation as catalysts for the Colorado push.

stories.headline: Solar farms occupy less than 1% of Colorado farmland despite rural opposition, industry study finds
stories.summary:
Ground-mounted solar projects covered just 14,326 acres across 39 Colorado counties at the end of 2024 — less than 1% of the state's 30.2 million acres of farmland — according to a study released by the Colorado Solar and Storage Association. The findings push back against fears that utility-scale solar development is consuming significant agricultural land in rural parts of the state.

The study found that between 1972 and 2000, roughly 2 million acres of Colorado farmland were converted to rural subdivisions, and that the ten counties accounting for 90% of farmland lost between 2017 and 2022 saw those losses driven primarily by suburban sprawl rather than solar development. 'Farmland isn't being lost to renewable energy, it's being lost to suburban sprawl,' said KC Becker, the association's CEO.

Despite the relatively small footprint, utility-scale solar projects have drawn significant opposition in several Colorado counties. Projects in Delta County, San Miguel County and La Plata County each faced community resistance, with some canceled or substantially modified. At least a dozen counties have imposed solar moratoriums while developing land-use regulations, and 39 counties have now adopted such rules.

In 2025, an additional 6,700 acres of solar projects were added statewide. The 2024 Colorado legislature passed a bill directing state agencies to evaluate local permitting processes after widespread local pushback threatened to slow the state's renewable energy buildout.

stories.headline: Colorado State Patrol and four metro agencies launch I-25 enforcement crackdown following 100-plus crashes in 2025
stories.summary:
Colorado State Patrol joined forces with Greenwood Village Police, Lone Tree Police, the Douglas County Sheriff's Office and the Arapahoe County Sheriff's Office Wednesday for a joint traffic enforcement operation along a 15-mile stretch of Interstate 25 between Castle Rock and Monument. The corridor recorded more than 100 crashes and three fatalities in 2025, prompting the coordinated response.

The primary focus of the operation was speeding, which CSP identifies as the third leading cause of traffic crashes in Colorado. In 2025, 31 people statewide were killed in crashes where speeding was cited as a causal factor, and CSP troopers issued 42,300 speeding citations across the state that year. Officers also enforced compliance with Colorado's 'Slow Down, Move Over' law.

Preliminary data from the Colorado Department of Transportation shows 701 traffic deaths statewide in 2025, a 2% increase over 2024. CDOT attributed much of the increase to an unseasonably warm late-year period that put more drivers on the road in November and December, after the state had been trending toward a 7% annual decline through October.

'We don't want people to think that we're out there to make revenue,' said CSP Sgt. Ivan Alvarado. 'What we care about is those 100-plus crashes, those three families that had to hear from us.' Officials said the enforcement effort is expected to continue with periodic joint operations along the corridor.

stories.headline: Mountain snowstorm drops over a foot at high elevations; Denver area sees no accumulation
stories.summary:
A winter storm swept through Colorado's high country between Tuesday and Thursday, depositing more than a foot of snow on the state's highest peaks and mountain passes, according to the National Weather Service. A winter storm warning covering parts of Jackson, Larimer, Grand and Boulder counties was in effect from 8 p.m. Tuesday through late Wednesday night, with wind gusts reaching up to 65 mph reducing visibility significantly along mountain passes.

A separate winter weather advisory applied to portions of Grand, Boulder, Gilpin, Clear Creek, Summit and Park counties, including Breckenridge, Winter Park, Berthoud Pass and the Mosquito and Gore mountain ranges. Heavy snow and blowing conditions created hazardous travel through much of the central and northern mountains during the core of the storm Wednesday.

The Denver metro area received no accumulation from the system, though temperatures dropped into the teens along the Interstate 25 corridor overnight Friday. Ski resorts across the state reported nearly a foot of fresh powder, with forecasters calling for favorable skiing and snowboarding conditions through the weekend.

Following the storm, a ridge of high pressure is expected to rebuild early next week, pushing Denver's daytime highs into the 60s. Forecasters noted the city could approach record high temperatures by Tuesday afternoon.

stories.headline: Buy Colorado Day, Dairy Block Petite Parade and Purim Drag Queen Bingo highlight Denver's weekend events
stories.summary:
Denver's annual Buy Colorado Day — formerly known as 303 Day and designated a state holiday in 2025 — returns Tuesday, March 3, with bars, restaurants and boutiques across the metro offering themed products and special pricing including $3.03 cocktails. Indie 102.3 FM is hosting a free all-ages party from 5 to 9 p.m. at Number Thirty Eight, featuring local artists Bruha, Graveyard Choir and Cheap Perfume.

The eighth annual Dairy Block Petite Parade takes place Sunday, March 1, in LoDo's heated alley on the 1800 block of Wazee Street. The free Mardi Gras-inspired event features miniature floats built on shoebox bases and runs from noon to 2 p.m., with post-parade entertainment and prizes for Most Original Float and Best Team Theme.

The Mizel Arts and Culture Center's sixth annual Purim Drag Queen Bingo, themed 'Garden of Eden,' is scheduled for Saturday, Feb. 28, from 7:30 to 10:30 p.m. at the Staenberg-Loup Jewish Community Center Social Hall at 250 S. Dahlia St. The 21-and-up event includes a costume contest, cash bar and vegetarian appetizers, with tickets ranging from $50 to $75.

Latinos Run Denver is also hosting a free all-abilities 5K, brunch and film screening on Sunday, March 1, beginning at 8:30 a.m. outside REI Denver at 1416 Platte St. The event benefits the Ray of Hope Cancer Foundation and includes a screening of the documentary 'The Life We Have' and a panel with local health advocates.

stories.headline: Colorado ski resorts report near-foot of fresh powder following midweek mountain storm
stories.summary:
Colorado's ski resorts received close to a foot of fresh snow from a midweek winter storm that moved through the high country between Tuesday and Thursday, according to National Weather Service data and local forecasters. The snowfall came as ski areas statewide had been contending with a below-average snow season marked by above-normal temperatures through much of the winter.

The storm, which brought a winter storm warning to parts of Jackson, Larimer, Grand and Boulder counties, produced the most significant snow accumulation of the season at many Front Range-accessible resorts. Wind gusts of up to 65 mph accompanied the snowfall, with the heaviest accumulations falling between Tuesday evening and Wednesday afternoon.

Forecasters described the weekend as ideal for skiing and snowboarding across the state following the storm's passage. The Denver metro, which saw no accumulation, is expected to see daytime highs in the mid- to upper 40s through the weekend before a warming trend pushes temperatures into the 60s early next week.

The storm provides a significant boost for the ski industry heading into March, historically Colorado's snowiest month. Resort operators and the broader mountain tourism economy — a major contributor to Colorado's state revenue — have been monitoring the season's below-average snowpack with concern.

stories.headline: Potential major Denver snowstorm in early March draws attention amid season of record warmth
stories.summary:
Long-range weather mapping from Pivotal Weather is showing the possibility of significant snowfall in Denver between March 5 and March 12, with one model projecting up to 14 inches of total accumulation over that period. The forecast has drawn attention given Denver's unusually warm and dry winter, though meteorologists caution that models diverge significantly at that range and some forecasting services show little to no snow for the city.

Pivotal Weather's mapping as of Feb. 26 showed a potential 10-inch accumulation between March 6 and March 8, with an extended period through March 12 bringing the total projection to 14.1 inches. AccuWeather's competing forecast calls for temperatures in the 50s and 60s during the same period with no significant snow, illustrating the wide uncertainty in the extended outlook.

Historically, March is Denver's snowiest month, and several of the city's largest single-day snowfalls on record have occurred in March, including 22.9 inches on March 18, 2003, and 19.9 inches on March 14, 2021. A storm of the projected magnitude would represent the largest snowfall of the 2025-26 season for the city.

Denver was on track to approach a record high temperature near 70 degrees by Tuesday, Feb. 24, before the eventual storm system arrives. Residents and transportation planners are watching the forecast closely given the significant travel and public safety implications a major early-March snowstorm would carry.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
